text: Ureteroenteric anastomosis stricture represents one of the principal complications after radical cystectomy and the main cause of renal dysfunction after urinary diversion. The gold standard treatment consists on open revision in most cases and can result with significant morbidity. And the robotic approach can be a minimally invasive treatment option for these patients.

We present the clinical case of a sixty-four-year-old patient with past history of muscle-invasive bladder cancer who underwent robotic radical cystectomy in June, twenty-twenty-two. The surgical technique for urinary diversion consists on an intracorporeal Y-shaped neobladder. Four months later, the patient presented with fever and bilateral lower back pain. The CT scan showed a mild left-sided ectasia up to the urethral enteric anastomosis and moderate right-sided ectasia with two centimeters of distal stenosis. The renogram showed proper function of both kidneys.

The patient is positioned in the supine and Trendelenburg position for robotic surgery. Conventional robotic trocars are placed. The surgeon begins by freeing pelvic adhesions and instills fluid through the urethral catheter to identify the bladder. Dissection of the left ureter is performed at its distal and proximal portions, revealing a short stenosis at the left ureteroenteric anastomosis.

The surgeon performs direct ureteral reimplantation using an exteriorized ureteral catheter. The right ureter is freed and dissected, revealing a stenosis. The ureter is confirmed to reach the bladder with tension.
The surgeon creates a new bladder flap and performs a body-type ureteral reimplantation.
The patient presented a postoperative period with no incidences. In conclusion, robotical approach results a feasible option in selected patients.
